However, during the Meiji Restoration, the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was identified with Goshirō Hachiman (now {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}}) in the same city. Therefore, the Shrine Inspector designated the formal name as "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha". According to the Seya Rigon (勢陽俚諺), "Kan-date" was sometimes written as "Kōtatsu". ==Deities enshrined== The following four kami are the primary enshrined deities: * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}} (Amaterasu-ōmikami) * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}} (Toyouke-ōmikami) * [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (Yamatohime-no-mikoto) * {{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} (Otokazuchi-no-mikoto, ancestor of the Iitaka county head) On November 10, 1906 (Meiji 39), the following three kami were merged into this shrine; their origins are unclear: * {{ill|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|de|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命|zh|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穗耳命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame no Oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}} * [[:en:Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi]] * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花開耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} ==History== The area was established as the *kanda* and *kōbe* of Iitaka District, as recorded in Engishiki, with two *chō* of kanda and 36 households of *kanbe*. According to the Kōtaijingū Gishikichō, during the reign of Emperor Suinin, Yamatohime-no-mikoto brought Amaterasu to this region, where the local clan head Otokazuchi-no-mikoto presented kanda and kōbe lands and built the Iino-takamimiya. The Yamato-hime Chronicle similarly notes that in Suinin 22, Yamatohime-no-mikoto worshipped the Great Kami at Iino-takamimiya for four years, receiving these lands from Otokazuchi-no-mikoto (ancestor of the Wani clan). From then on, offerings were sent to Ise Grand Shrine. The 1906 Shrine Ledger states that a shrine hall was constructed at this site to store these offerings; the Kōbe Office was established to administer local affairs, and Shinmei-sha was enshrined, with the old Kōbe villagers repeatedly rebuilding the shrine. According to the 1952 Shrine Ledger, in 1382 (Kōwa 2), Kitabatake Akiyasu donated four *koku* of shrine lands, a grant upheld by the Kii Domain during the Edo period, but abolished after the Meiji Restoration. A pond on the grounds was once harvested for rushes used to make mats, annually dedicated to Ise Grand Shrine. In the early Meiji period, it was renamed "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha" and ranked as a village shrine. ===Identification as an Engishiki shrine=== The earlier ledger records that it was called "Ohi Shrine". The first to identify it as the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was Deguchi Nobumichi’s *Shinmyōchō Kōshō*, citing the "Shimohino Ogawa" river east of the shrine as the "Shimohino Ogawa" in Engishiki. Because "hino" and "hi" are phonetically similar, this was argued as evidence. Conversely, Imamori Kiyonao in the *Ise Shikinaisha Kenroku* argued that "Shimohino Ogawa" refers to the Atago River flowing west of Kaki-hana Village, not east, and that "Ohi" has no relation to Shimohino. He noted that shrine offices for each of the six districts of Ise Province used the name "Kan-date Shrine" to enshrine the spirit of Ise Grand Shrine, and since Otokazuchi-no-mikoto appears among the enshrined kami here, this shrine served as the guardian for Iitaka’s kōbe and should be identified as a Kokutsu Shrine. He accordingly identified the Engishiki Ohi Shrine with the current Matsusaka Shrine instead. ==Shrine buildings and subsidiary shrines== The main hall is built in the Shinmei-zukuri style. Other structures include the worship hall and office. A _reisha_ shrine dedicated to the war dead from the Meiji Restoration through the Greater East Asia War is located on the grounds. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku'', vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1. Kōgakkan University Press, 1990. ==See also== {{commonscat|Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Ohi Shrine: ** {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]
{{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (minor) · former village shrine | established = unknown | shrine_architecture = Shinmei-zukuri | annual_festival = March 28 }} '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Matsusaka City, Mie Prefecture, Japan. It is one of the claimants to the Engishiki-listed "Ohi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, and is traditionally identified as a former Ise shrine site (Moto Ise) where Yamatohime-no-mikoto temporarily stayed at Iino-takamimiya while searching for the place to enshrine Amaterasu. Its former shrine rank was that of a village shrine. ==Shrine name== Historically called "Kan-date Shrine", the Shrine Ledger of 1952 (Shōwa 27) notes that it was also called "Ohi Shrine". However, during the Meiji Restoration, the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was identified with Goshirō Hachiman (now {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}}) in the same city. Therefore, the Shrine Inspector designated the formal name as "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha". According to the Seya Rigon (勢陽俚諺), "Kan-date" was sometimes written as "Kōtatsu". ==Deities enshrined== The following four kami are the primary enshrined deities: * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}} (Amaterasu-ōmikami) * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}} (Toyouke-ōmikami) * [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (Yamatohime-no-mikoto) * {{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} (Otokazuchi-no-mikoto, ancestor of the Iitaka county head) On November 10, 1906 (Meiji 39), the following three kami were merged into this shrine; their origins are unclear: * {{ill|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|de|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命|zh|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穗耳命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame no Oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}} * [[:en:Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi]] * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花開耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} ==History== The area was established as the *kanda* and *kōbe* of Iitaka District, as recorded in Engishiki, with two *chō* of kanda and 36 households of *kanbe*. According to the Kōtaijingū Gishikichō, during the reign of Emperor Suinin, Yamatohime-no-mikoto brought Amaterasu to this region, where the local clan head Otokazuchi-no-mikoto presented kanda and kōbe lands and built the Iino-takamimiya. The Yamato-hime Chronicle similarly notes that in Suinin 22, Yamatohime-no-mikoto worshipped the Great Kami at Iino-takamimiya for four years, receiving these lands from Otokazuchi-no-mikoto (ancestor of the Wani clan). From then on, offerings were sent to Ise Grand Shrine. The 1906 Shrine Ledger states that a shrine hall was constructed at this site to store these offerings; the Kōbe Office was established to administer local affairs, and Shinmei-sha was enshrined, with the old Kōbe villagers repeatedly rebuilding the shrine. According to the 1952 Shrine Ledger, in 1382 (Kōwa 2), Kitabatake Akiyasu donated four *koku* of shrine lands, a grant upheld by the Kii Domain during the Edo period, but abolished after the Meiji Restoration. The first one was {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]). ==Overview== {{Shinto shrine | name = '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) | image = [[File:Le Temple Shintô Kanbe-kôtachi-shin'mei-sha - Le bâtiment principal "Le honden".jpg|290px]]
Main hall | location = 1791 Shimomura-chō, Matsusaka, Mie Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|11.4|N|136|32|59.6|E|region:JP-24|type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}}
{{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}}
[[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]]
{{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (minor) · former village shrine | established = unknown | shrine_architecture = Shinmei-zukuri | annual_festival = March 28 }} '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Matsusaka City, Mie Prefecture, Japan. It is one of the claimants to the Engishiki-listed "Ohi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, and is traditionally identified as a former Ise shrine site (Moto Ise) where Yamatohime-no-mikoto temporarily stayed at Iino-takamimiya while searching for the place to enshrine Amaterasu. Its former shrine rank was that of a village shrine. ==Shrine name== Historically called "Kan-date Shrine", the Shrine Ledger of 1952 (Shōwa 27) notes that it was also called "Ohi Shrine". However, during the Meiji Restoration, the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was identified with Goshirō Hachiman (now {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}}) in the same city. Therefore, the Shrine Inspector designated the formal name as "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha". According to the Seya Rigon (勢陽俚諺), "Kan-date" was sometimes written as "Kōtatsu". ==Deities enshrined== The following four kami are the primary enshrined deities: * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}} (Amaterasu-ōmikami) * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}} (Toyouke-ōmikami) * [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (Yamatohime-no-mikoto) * {{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} (Otokazuchi-no-mikoto, ancestor of the Iitaka county head) On November 10, 1906 (Meiji 39), the following three kami were merged into this shrine; their origins are unclear: * {{ill|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|de|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命|zh|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穗耳命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame no Oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}} * [[:en:Ōyamatsumi|Ōyamatsumi]] * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花開耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} ==History== The area was established as the *kanda* and *kōbe* of Iitaka District, as recorded in Engishiki, with two *chō* of kanda and 36 households of *kanbe*. According to the Kōtaijingū Gishikichō, during the reign of Emperor Suinin, Yamatohime-no-mikoto brought Amaterasu to this region, where the local clan head Otokazuchi-no-mikoto presented kanda and kōbe lands and built the Iino-takamimiya. The Yamato-hime Chronicle similarly notes that in Suinin 22, Yamatohime-no-mikoto worshipped the Great Kami at Iino-takamimiya for four years, receiving these lands from Otokazuchi-no-mikoto (ancestor of the Wani clan). From then on, offerings were sent to Ise Grand Shrine. The 1906 Shrine Ledger states that a shrine hall was constructed at this site to store these offerings; the Kōbe Office was established to administer local affairs, and Shinmei-sha was enshrined, with the old Kōbe villagers repeatedly rebuilding the shrine. According to the 1952 Shrine Ledger, in 1382 (Kōwa 2), Kitabatake Akiyasu donated four *koku* of shrine lands, a grant upheld by the Kii Domain during the Edo period, but abolished after the Meiji Restoration. A pond on the grounds was once harvested for rushes used to make mats, annually dedicated to Ise Grand Shrine. In the early Meiji period, it was renamed "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha" and ranked as a village shrine. ===Identification as an Engishiki shrine=== The earlier ledger records that it was called "Ohi Shrine". The first to identify it as the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was Deguchi Nobumichi’s *Shinmyōchō Kōshō*, citing the "Shimohino Ogawa" river east of the shrine as the "Shimohino Ogawa" in Engishiki. Because "hino" and "hi" are phonetically similar, this was argued as evidence. Conversely, Imamori Kiyonao in the *Ise Shikinaisha Kenroku* argued that "Shimohino Ogawa" refers to the Atago River flowing west of Kaki-hana Village, not east, and that "Ohi" has no relation to Shimohino. He noted that shrine offices for each of the six districts of Ise Province used the name "Kan-date Shrine" to enshrine the spirit of Ise Grand Shrine, and since Otokazuchi-no-mikoto appears among the enshrined kami here, this shrine served as the guardian for Iitaka’s kōbe and should be identified as a Kokutsu Shrine. He accordingly identified the Engishiki Ohi Shrine with the current Matsusaka Shrine instead. ==Shrine buildings and subsidiary shrines== The main hall is built in the Shinmei-zukuri style. Other structures include the worship hall and office. A _reisha_ shrine dedicated to the war dead from the Meiji Restoration through the Greater East Asia War is located on the grounds. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku'', vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1. Kōgakkan University Press, 1990. ==See also== {{commonscat|Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Ohi Shrine: ** {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Kunitsu Shrine: ** {{ill|Kunitotomi Shrine|de|Kunitotomi-Schrein|ja|加世智神社|zh|加世智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitotomi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579678}} (merged into Kunitsu Shrine in Ōhirao-chō, Matsusaka) * Former Ise shrine sites (Iino-takamimiya): ** {{ill|Kamiyama Shrine|de|Kamiyama-Schrein|ja|神山神社|zh|神山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyama Shrine|1=kouyamajinja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17503039}} (Yamazoe-chō, Matsusaka) ** {{ill|Kunitsu Shrine|de|Kunitsu-Schrein|ja|久尓都神社|zh|久尓都神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579680}} ** {{ill|Takino Shinmei Shrine|de|Takino-Shinmei-Schrein|ja|水屋神社|zh|水屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takino Shinmei Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579681}} (merged into Mizuya Shrine, Iitaka) ** {{ill|Hanaoka Shrine|de|Hanaoka-Schrein|ja|花岡神社|zh|花岡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanaoka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579682}} [[ja:神戸神館神明社]] It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha|Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} [[Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Motoise shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} Moto-Ise Shrines are {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. The first one was {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ({{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}). ==Overview== {{Shinto shrine | name = '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) | image = [[File:Le Temple Shintô Kanbe-kôtachi-shin'mei-sha - Le bâtiment principal "Le honden".jpg|290px]]
Main hall | location = 1791 Shimomura-chō, Matsusaka, Mie Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|11.4|N|136|32|59.6|E|region:JP-24|type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}}
{{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}}
{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}
{{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (minor) · former village shrine | established = unknown | shrine_architecture = Shinmei-zukuri | annual_festival = March 28 }} '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Matsusaka City, Mie Prefecture, Japan. It is one of the claimants to the Engishiki-listed "Ohi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, and is traditionally identified as a former Ise shrine site (Moto Ise) where Yamatohime-no-mikoto temporarily stayed at Iino-takamimiya while searching for the place to enshrine Amaterasu. Its former shrine rank was that of a village shrine. ==Shrine name== Historically called "Kan-date Shrine", the Shrine Ledger of 1952 (Shōwa 27) notes that it was also called "Ohi Shrine". However, during the Meiji Restoration, the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was identified with Goshirō Hachiman (now {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}}) in the same city. Therefore, the Shrine Inspector designated the formal name as "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha". According to the Seya Rigon (勢陽俚諺), "Kan-date" was sometimes written as "Kōtatsu". ==Deities enshrined== The following four kami are the primary enshrined deities: * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}} (Amaterasu-ōmikami) * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}} (Toyouke-ōmikami) * {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} (Yamatohime-no-mikoto) * {{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} (Otokazuchi-no-mikoto, ancestor of the Iitaka county head) On November 10, 1906 (Meiji 39), the following three kami were merged into this shrine; their origins are unclear: * {{ill|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|de|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命|zh|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穗耳命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame no Oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花開耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} ==History== The area was established as the *kanda* and *kōbe* of Iitaka District, as recorded in Engishiki, with two *chō* of kanda and 36 households of *kanbe*. According to the Kōtaijingū Gishikichō, during the reign of Emperor Suinin, Yamatohime-no-mikoto brought Amaterasu to this region, where the local clan head Otokazuchi-no-mikoto presented kanda and kōbe lands and built the Iino-takamimiya. The Yamato-hime Chronicle similarly notes that in Suinin 22, Yamatohime-no-mikoto worshipped the Great Kami at Iino-takamimiya for four years, receiving these lands from Otokazuchi-no-mikoto (ancestor of the Wani clan). From then on, offerings were sent to Ise Grand Shrine. The 1906 Shrine Ledger states that a shrine hall was constructed at this site to store these offerings; the Kōbe Office was established to administer local affairs, and Shinmei-sha was enshrined, with the old Kōbe villagers repeatedly rebuilding the shrine. According to the 1952 Shrine Ledger, in 1382 (Kōwa 2), Kitabatake Akiyasu donated four *koku* of shrine lands, a grant upheld by the Kii Domain during the Edo period, but abolished after the Meiji Restoration. A pond on the grounds was once harvested for rushes used to make mats, annually dedicated to Ise Grand Shrine. In the early Meiji period, it was renamed "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha" and ranked as a village shrine. ===Identification as an Engishiki shrine=== The earlier ledger records that it was called "Ohi Shrine". The first to identify it as the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was Deguchi Nobumichi’s *Shinmyōchō Kōshō*, citing the "Shimohino Ogawa" river east of the shrine as the "Shimohino Ogawa" in Engishiki. Because "hino" and "hi" are phonetically similar, this was argued as evidence. Conversely, Imamori Kiyonao in the *Ise Shikinaisha Kenroku* argued that "Shimohino Ogawa" refers to the Atago River flowing west of Kaki-hana Village, not east, and that "Ohi" has no relation to Shimohino. He noted that shrine offices for each of the six districts of Ise Province used the name "Kan-date Shrine" to enshrine the spirit of Ise Grand Shrine, and since Otokazuchi-no-mikoto appears among the enshrined kami here, this shrine served as the guardian for Iitaka’s kōbe and should be identified as a Kokutsu Shrine. He accordingly identified the Engishiki Ohi Shrine with the current Matsusaka Shrine instead. ==Shrine buildings and subsidiary shrines== The main hall is built in the Shinmei-zukuri style. Other structures include the worship hall and office. A _reisha_ shrine dedicated to the war dead from the Meiji Restoration through the Greater East Asia War is located on the grounds. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku'', vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1. Kōgakkan University Press, 1990. ==See also== {{commonscat|Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Ohi Shrine: ** {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Kunitsu Shrine: ** {{ill|Kunitotomi Shrine|de|Kunitotomi-Schrein|ja|加世智神社|zh|加世智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitotomi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579678}} (merged into Kunitsu Shrine in Ōhirao-chō, Matsusaka) * Former Ise shrine sites (Iino-takamimiya): ** {{ill|Kamiyama Shrine|de|Kamiyama-Schrein|ja|神山神社|zh|神山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyama Shrine|1=kouyamajinja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17503039}} (Yamazoe-chō, Matsusaka) ** {{ill|Kunitsu Shrine|de|Kunitsu-Schrein|ja|久尓都神社|zh|久尓都神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579680}} ** {{ill|Takino Shinmei Shrine|de|Takino-Shinmei-Schrein|ja|水屋神社|zh|水屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takino Shinmei Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579681}} (merged into Mizuya Shrine, Iitaka) ** {{ill|Hanaoka Shrine|de|Hanaoka-Schrein|ja|花岡神社|zh|花岡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanaoka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579682}} [[ja:神戸神館神明社]] [[de:Kanbe-Kotachi-Shinmei-Schrein]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|神戸神館神明社|version=88389931|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{everybodywiki link|Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} [[ja:%E7%A5%9E%E6%88%B8%E7%A5%9E%E9%A4%A8%E7%A5%9E%E6%98%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Commons category|Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha}} {{wikidata link|Q11590193}} [[Category:Buildings in Matsusaka, Mie]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]] [[Category:Everybody linked]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] According to the Kōtaijingū Gishikichō, during the reign of Emperor Suinin, Yamatohime-no-mikoto brought Amaterasu to this region, where the local clan head Otokazuchi-no-mikoto presented kanda and kōbe lands and built the Iino-takamimiya. From then on, offerings were sent to Ise Grand Shrine. The 1906 Shrine Ledger states that a shrine hall was constructed at this site to store these offerings; the Kōbe Office was established to administer local affairs, and Shinmei-sha was enshrined, with the old Kōbe villagers repeatedly rebuilding the shrine. According to the 1952 Shrine Ledger, in 1382 (Kōwa 2), Kitabatake Akiyasu donated four *koku* of shrine lands, a grant upheld by the Kii Domain during the Edo period, but abolished after the Meiji Restoration. A pond on the grounds was once harvested for rushes used to make mats, annually dedicated to Ise Grand Shrine. In the early Meiji period, it was renamed "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha" and ranked as a village shrine. ===Identification as an Engishiki shrine=== The earlier ledger records that it was called "Ohi Shrine". The first to identify it as the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was Deguchi Nobumichi’s *Shinmyōchō Kōshō*, citing the "Shimohino Ogawa" river east of the shrine as the "Shimohino Ogawa" in Engishiki. Because "hino" and "hi" are phonetically similar, this was argued as evidence. Conversely, Imamori Kiyonao in the *Ise Shikinaisha Kenroku* argued that "Shimohino Ogawa" refers to the Atago River flowing west of Kaki-hana Village, not east, and that "Ohi" has no relation to Shimohino. He noted that shrine offices for each of the six districts of Ise Province used the name "Kan-date Shrine" to enshrine the spirit of Ise Grand Shrine, and since Otokazuchi-no-mikoto appears among the enshrined kami here, this shrine served as the guardian for Iitaka’s kōbe and should be identified as a Kokutsu Shrine. He accordingly identified the Engishiki Ohi Shrine with the current Matsusaka Shrine instead. ==Shrine buildings and subsidiary shrines== The main hall is built in the Shinmei-zukuri style. Other structures include the worship hall and office. A _reisha_ shrine dedicated to the war dead from the Meiji Restoration through the Greater East Asia War is located on the grounds. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. The first one was {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ({{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}). ==Overview== {{Shinto shrine | name = '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) | image = [[File:Le Temple Shintô Kanbe-kôtachi-shin'mei-sha - Le bâtiment principal "Le honden".jpg|290px]]
Main hall | location = 1791 Shimomura-chō, Matsusaka, Mie Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|11.4|N|136|32|59.6|E|region:JP-24|type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}}
{{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}}
{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}
{{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (minor) · former village shrine | established = unknown | shrine_architecture = Shinmei-zukuri | annual_festival = March 28 }} '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Matsusaka City, Mie Prefecture, Japan. It is one of the claimants to the Engishiki-listed "Ohi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, and is traditionally identified as a former Ise shrine site (Moto Ise) where Yamatohime-no-mikoto temporarily stayed at Iino-takamimiya while searching for the place to enshrine Amaterasu. Its former shrine rank was that of a village shrine. ==Shrine name== Historically called "Kan-date Shrine", the Shrine Ledger of 1952 (Shōwa 27) notes that it was also called "Ohi Shrine". However, during the Meiji Restoration, the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was identified with Goshirō Hachiman (now {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}}) in the same city. Therefore, the Shrine Inspector designated the formal name as "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha". According to the Seya Rigon (勢陽俚諺), "Kan-date" was sometimes written as "Kōtatsu". ==Deities enshrined== The following four kami are the primary enshrined deities: * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}} (Amaterasu-ōmikami) * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}} (Toyouke-ōmikami) * {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} (Yamatohime-no-mikoto) * {{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} (Otokazuchi-no-mikoto, ancestor of the Iitaka county head) On November 10, 1906 (Meiji 39), the following three kami were merged into this shrine; their origins are unclear: * {{ill|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|de|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命|zh|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穗耳命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame no Oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花開耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} ==History== The area was established as the *kanda* and *kōbe* of Iitaka District, as recorded in Engishiki, with two *chō* of kanda and 36 households of *kanbe*. According to the Kōtaijingū Gishikichō, during the reign of Emperor Suinin, Yamatohime-no-mikoto brought Amaterasu to this region, where the local clan head Otokazuchi-no-mikoto presented kanda and kōbe lands and built the Iino-takamimiya. The Yamato-hime Chronicle similarly notes that in Suinin 22, Yamatohime-no-mikoto worshipped the Great Kami at Iino-takamimiya for four years, receiving these lands from Otokazuchi-no-mikoto (ancestor of the Wani clan). From then on, offerings were sent to Ise Grand Shrine. The 1906 Shrine Ledger states that a shrine hall was constructed at this site to store these offerings; the Kōbe Office was established to administer local affairs, and Shinmei-sha was enshrined, with the old Kōbe villagers repeatedly rebuilding the shrine. According to the 1952 Shrine Ledger, in 1382 (Kōwa 2), Kitabatake Akiyasu donated four *koku* of shrine lands, a grant upheld by the Kii Domain during the Edo period, but abolished after the Meiji Restoration. A pond on the grounds was once harvested for rushes used to make mats, annually dedicated to Ise Grand Shrine. In the early Meiji period, it was renamed "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha" and ranked as a village shrine. ===Identification as an Engishiki shrine=== The earlier ledger records that it was called "Ohi Shrine". The first to identify it as the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was Deguchi Nobumichi’s *Shinmyōchō Kōshō*, citing the "Shimohino Ogawa" river east of the shrine as the "Shimohino Ogawa" in Engishiki. Because "hino" and "hi" are phonetically similar, this was argued as evidence. Conversely, Imamori Kiyonao in the *Ise Shikinaisha Kenroku* argued that "Shimohino Ogawa" refers to the Atago River flowing west of Kaki-hana Village, not east, and that "Ohi" has no relation to Shimohino. He noted that shrine offices for each of the six districts of Ise Province used the name "Kan-date Shrine" to enshrine the spirit of Ise Grand Shrine, and since Otokazuchi-no-mikoto appears among the enshrined kami here, this shrine served as the guardian for Iitaka’s kōbe and should be identified as a Kokutsu Shrine. He accordingly identified the Engishiki Ohi Shrine with the current Matsusaka Shrine instead. ==Shrine buildings and subsidiary shrines== The main hall is built in the Shinmei-zukuri style. Other structures include the worship hall and office. A _reisha_ shrine dedicated to the war dead from the Meiji Restoration through the Greater East Asia War is located on the grounds. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku'', vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1. However, during the Meiji Restoration, the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was identified with Goshirō Hachiman (now {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}}) in the same city. Therefore, the Shrine Inspector designated the formal name as "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha". According to the Seya Rigon (勢陽俚諺), "Kan-date" was sometimes written as "Kōtatsu". ==Deities enshrined== The following four kami are the primary enshrined deities: * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}} (Amaterasu-ōmikami) * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}} (Toyouke-ōmikami) * {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} (Yamatohime-no-mikoto) * {{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} (Otokazuchi-no-mikoto, ancestor of the Iitaka county head) On November 10, 1906 (Meiji 39), the following three kami were merged into this shrine; their origins are unclear: * {{ill|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|de|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命|zh|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穗耳命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame no Oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花開耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} ==History== The area was established as the *kanda* and *kōbe* of Iitaka District, as recorded in Engishiki, with two *chō* of kanda and 36 households of *kanbe*. According to the Kōtaijingū Gishikichō, during the reign of Emperor Suinin, Yamatohime-no-mikoto brought Amaterasu to this region, where the local clan head Otokazuchi-no-mikoto presented kanda and kōbe lands and built the Iino-takamimiya. The Yamato-hime Chronicle similarly notes that in Suinin 22, Yamatohime-no-mikoto worshipped the Great Kami at Iino-takamimiya for four years, receiving these lands from Otokazuchi-no-mikoto (ancestor of the Wani clan). From then on, offerings were sent to Ise Grand Shrine. The 1906 Shrine Ledger states that a shrine hall was constructed at this site to store these offerings; the Kōbe Office was established to administer local affairs, and Shinmei-sha was enshrined, with the old Kōbe villagers repeatedly rebuilding the shrine. According to the 1952 Shrine Ledger, in 1382 (Kōwa 2), Kitabatake Akiyasu donated four *koku* of shrine lands, a grant upheld by the Kii Domain during the Edo period, but abolished after the Meiji Restoration. A pond on the grounds was once harvested for rushes used to make mats, annually dedicated to Ise Grand Shrine. In the early Meiji period, it was renamed "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha" and ranked as a village shrine. ===Identification as an Engishiki shrine=== The earlier ledger records that it was called "Ohi Shrine". The first to identify it as the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was Deguchi Nobumichi’s *Shinmyōchō Kōshō*, citing the "Shimohino Ogawa" river east of the shrine as the "Shimohino Ogawa" in Engishiki. Because "hino" and "hi" are phonetically similar, this was argued as evidence. Conversely, Imamori Kiyonao in the *Ise Shikinaisha Kenroku* argued that "Shimohino Ogawa" refers to the Atago River flowing west of Kaki-hana Village, not east, and that "Ohi" has no relation to Shimohino. He noted that shrine offices for each of the six districts of Ise Province used the name "Kan-date Shrine" to enshrine the spirit of Ise Grand Shrine, and since Otokazuchi-no-mikoto appears among the enshrined kami here, this shrine served as the guardian for Iitaka’s kōbe and should be identified as a Kokutsu Shrine. He accordingly identified the Engishiki Ohi Shrine with the current Matsusaka Shrine instead. ==Shrine buildings and subsidiary shrines== The main hall is built in the Shinmei-zukuri style. Other structures include the worship hall and office. A _reisha_ shrine dedicated to the war dead from the Meiji Restoration through the Greater East Asia War is located on the grounds. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku'', vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1. Kōgakkan University Press, 1990. ==See also== {{commonscat|Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Ohi Shrine: ** {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Kunitsu Shrine: ** {{ill|Kunitotomi Shrine|de|Kunitotomi-Schrein|ja|加世智神社|zh|加世智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitotomi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579678}} (merged into Kunitsu Shrine in Ōhirao-chō, Matsusaka) * Former Ise shrine sites (Iino-takamimiya): ** {{ill|Kamiyama Shrine|de|Kamiyama-Schrein|ja|神山神社|zh|神山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyama Shrine|1=kouyamajinja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17503039}} (Yamazoe-chō, Matsusaka) ** {{ill|Kunitsu Shrine|de|Kunitsu-Schrein|ja|久尓都神社|zh|久尓都神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579680}} ** {{ill|Takino Shinmei Shrine|de|Takino-Shinmei-Schrein|ja|水屋神社|zh|水屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takino Shinmei Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579681}} (merged into Mizuya Shrine, Iitaka) ** {{ill|Hanaoka Shrine|de|Hanaoka-Schrein|ja|花岡神社|zh|花岡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanaoka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579682}} [[ja:神戸神館神明社]] {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}
{{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (minor) · former village shrine | established = unknown | shrine_architecture = Shinmei-zukuri | annual_festival = March 28 }} '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Matsusaka City, Mie Prefecture, Japan. It is one of the claimants to the Engishiki-listed "Ohi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, and is traditionally identified as a former Ise shrine site (Moto Ise) where Yamatohime-no-mikoto temporarily stayed at Iino-takamimiya while searching for the place to enshrine Amaterasu. Its former shrine rank was that of a village shrine. ==Shrine name== Historically called "Kan-date Shrine", the Shrine Ledger of 1952 (Shōwa 27) notes that it was also called "Ohi Shrine". However, during the Meiji Restoration, the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was identified with Goshirō Hachiman (now {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}}) in the same city. Therefore, the Shrine Inspector designated the formal name as "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha". According to the Seya Rigon (勢陽俚諺), "Kan-date" was sometimes written as "Kōtatsu". ==Deities enshrined== The following four kami are the primary enshrined deities: * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}} (Amaterasu-ōmikami) * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}} (Toyouke-ōmikami) * {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} (Yamatohime-no-mikoto) * {{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} (Otokazuchi-no-mikoto, ancestor of the Iitaka county head) On November 10, 1906 (Meiji 39), the following three kami were merged into this shrine; their origins are unclear: * {{ill|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|de|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命|zh|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穗耳命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame no Oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花開耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} ==History== The area was established as the *kanda* and *kōbe* of Iitaka District, as recorded in Engishiki, with two *chō* of kanda and 36 households of *kanbe*. {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}
{{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (minor) · former village shrine | established = unknown | shrine_architecture = Shinmei-zukuri | annual_festival = March 28 }} '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Matsusaka City, Mie Prefecture, Japan. It is one of the claimants to the Engishiki-listed "Ohi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, and is traditionally identified as a former Ise shrine site (Moto Ise) where Yamatohime-no-mikoto temporarily stayed at Iino-takamimiya while searching for the place to enshrine Amaterasu. Its former shrine rank was that of a village shrine. ==Shrine name== Historically called "Kan-date Shrine", the Shrine Ledger of 1952 (Shōwa 27) notes that it was also called "Ohi Shrine". However, during the Meiji Restoration, the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was identified with Goshirō Hachiman (now {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}}) in the same city. Therefore, the Shrine Inspector designated the formal name as "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha". According to the Seya Rigon (勢陽俚諺), "Kan-date" was sometimes written as "Kōtatsu". ==Deities enshrined== The following four kami are the primary enshrined deities: * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}} (Amaterasu-ōmikami) * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}} (Toyouke-ōmikami) * {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} (Yamatohime-no-mikoto) * {{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} (Otokazuchi-no-mikoto, ancestor of the Iitaka county head) On November 10, 1906 (Meiji 39), the following three kami were merged into this shrine; their origins are unclear: * {{ill|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|de|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命|zh|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穗耳命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame no Oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花開耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} ==History== The area was established as the *kanda* and *kōbe* of Iitaka District, as recorded in Engishiki, with two *chō* of kanda and 36 households of *kanbe*. According to the Kōtaijingū Gishikichō, during the reign of Emperor Suinin, Yamatohime-no-mikoto brought Amaterasu to this region, where the local clan head Otokazuchi-no-mikoto presented kanda and kōbe lands and built the Iino-takamimiya. The Yamato-hime Chronicle similarly notes that in Suinin 22, Yamatohime-no-mikoto worshipped the Great Kami at Iino-takamimiya for four years, receiving these lands from Otokazuchi-no-mikoto (ancestor of the Wani clan). From then on, offerings were sent to Ise Grand Shrine. The 1906 Shrine Ledger states that a shrine hall was constructed at this site to store these offerings; the Kōbe Office was established to administer local affairs, and Shinmei-sha was enshrined, with the old Kōbe villagers repeatedly rebuilding the shrine. According to the 1952 Shrine Ledger, in 1382 (Kōwa 2), Kitabatake Akiyasu donated four *koku* of shrine lands, a grant upheld by the Kii Domain during the Edo period, but abolished after the Meiji Restoration. A pond on the grounds was once harvested for rushes used to make mats, annually dedicated to Ise Grand Shrine. In the early Meiji period, it was renamed "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha" and ranked as a village shrine. ===Identification as an Engishiki shrine=== The earlier ledger records that it was called "Ohi Shrine". The first to identify it as the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was Deguchi Nobumichi’s *Shinmyōchō Kōshō*, citing the "Shimohino Ogawa" river east of the shrine as the "Shimohino Ogawa" in Engishiki. Because "hino" and "hi" are phonetically similar, this was argued as evidence. Conversely, Imamori Kiyonao in the *Ise Shikinaisha Kenroku* argued that "Shimohino Ogawa" refers to the Atago River flowing west of Kaki-hana Village, not east, and that "Ohi" has no relation to Shimohino. He noted that shrine offices for each of the six districts of Ise Province used the name "Kan-date Shrine" to enshrine the spirit of Ise Grand Shrine, and since Otokazuchi-no-mikoto appears among the enshrined kami here, this shrine served as the guardian for Iitaka’s kōbe and should be identified as a Kokutsu Shrine. He accordingly identified the Engishiki Ohi Shrine with the current Matsusaka Shrine instead. ==Shrine buildings and subsidiary shrines== The main hall is built in the Shinmei-zukuri style. Other structures include the worship hall and office. A _reisha_ shrine dedicated to the war dead from the Meiji Restoration through the Greater East Asia War is located on the grounds. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku'', vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1. Kōgakkan University Press, 1990. ==See also== {{commonscat|Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Ohi Shrine: ** {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Kunitsu Shrine: ** {{ill|Kunitotomi Shrine|de|Kunitotomi-Schrein|ja|加世智神社|zh|加世智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitotomi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579678}} (merged into Kunitsu Shrine in Ōhirao-chō, Matsusaka) * Former Ise shrine sites (Iino-takamimiya): ** {{ill|Kamiyama Shrine|de|Kamiyama-Schrein|ja|神山神社|zh|神山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyama Shrine|1=kouyamajinja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17503039}} (Yamazoe-chō, Matsusaka) ** {{ill|Kunitsu Shrine|de|Kunitsu-Schrein|ja|久尓都神社|zh|久尓都神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579680}} ** {{ill|Takino Shinmei Shrine|de|Takino-Shinmei-Schrein|ja|水屋神社|zh|水屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takino Shinmei Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579681}} (merged into Mizuya Shrine, Iitaka) ** {{ill|Hanaoka Shrine|de|Hanaoka-Schrein|ja|花岡神社|zh|花岡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanaoka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579682}} [[ja:神戸神館神明社]] Main hall | location = 1791 Shimomura-chō, Matsusaka, Mie Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|11.4|N|136|32|59.6|E|region:JP-24|type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}}
{{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}}
{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}
{{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (minor) · former village shrine | established = unknown | shrine_architecture = Shinmei-zukuri | annual_festival = March 28 }} '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Matsusaka City, Mie Prefecture, Japan. It is one of the claimants to the Engishiki-listed "Ohi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, and is traditionally identified as a former Ise shrine site (Moto Ise) where Yamatohime-no-mikoto temporarily stayed at Iino-takamimiya while searching for the place to enshrine Amaterasu. Its former shrine rank was that of a village shrine. ==Shrine name== Historically called "Kan-date Shrine", the Shrine Ledger of 1952 (Shōwa 27) notes that it was also called "Ohi Shrine". However, during the Meiji Restoration, the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was identified with Goshirō Hachiman (now {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}}) in the same city. Therefore, the Shrine Inspector designated the formal name as "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha". According to the Seya Rigon (勢陽俚諺), "Kan-date" was sometimes written as "Kōtatsu". ==Deities enshrined== The following four kami are the primary enshrined deities: * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}} (Amaterasu-ōmikami) * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}} (Toyouke-ōmikami) * {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} (Yamatohime-no-mikoto) * {{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677}} (Otokazuchi-no-mikoto, ancestor of the Iitaka county head) On November 10, 1906 (Meiji 39), the following three kami were merged into this shrine; their origins are unclear: * {{ill|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|de|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命|zh|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穗耳命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame no Oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花開耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} ==History== The area was established as the *kanda* and *kōbe* of Iitaka District, as recorded in Engishiki, with two *chō* of kanda and 36 households of *kanbe*. According to the Kōtaijingū Gishikichō, during the reign of Emperor Suinin, Yamatohime-no-mikoto brought Amaterasu to this region, where the local clan head Otokazuchi-no-mikoto presented kanda and kōbe lands and built the Iino-takamimiya. The Yamato-hime Chronicle similarly notes that in Suinin 22, Yamatohime-no-mikoto worshipped the Great Kami at Iino-takamimiya for four years, receiving these lands from Otokazuchi-no-mikoto (ancestor of the Wani clan). From then on, offerings were sent to Ise Grand Shrine. The 1906 Shrine Ledger states that a shrine hall was constructed at this site to store these offerings; the Kōbe Office was established to administer local affairs, and Shinmei-sha was enshrined, with the old Kōbe villagers repeatedly rebuilding the shrine. According to the 1952 Shrine Ledger, in 1382 (Kōwa 2), Kitabatake Akiyasu donated four *koku* of shrine lands, a grant upheld by the Kii Domain during the Edo period, but abolished after the Meiji Restoration. A pond on the grounds was once harvested for rushes used to make mats, annually dedicated to Ise Grand Shrine. In the early Meiji period, it was renamed "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha" and ranked as a village shrine. ===Identification as an Engishiki shrine=== The earlier ledger records that it was called "Ohi Shrine". The first to identify it as the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was Deguchi Nobumichi’s *Shinmyōchō Kōshō*, citing the "Shimohino Ogawa" river east of the shrine as the "Shimohino Ogawa" in Engishiki. Because "hino" and "hi" are phonetically similar, this was argued as evidence. Conversely, Imamori Kiyonao in the *Ise Shikinaisha Kenroku* argued that "Shimohino Ogawa" refers to the Atago River flowing west of Kaki-hana Village, not east, and that "Ohi" has no relation to Shimohino. He noted that shrine offices for each of the six districts of Ise Province used the name "Kan-date Shrine" to enshrine the spirit of Ise Grand Shrine, and since Otokazuchi-no-mikoto appears among the enshrined kami here, this shrine served as the guardian for Iitaka’s kōbe and should be identified as a Kokutsu Shrine. He accordingly identified the Engishiki Ohi Shrine with the current Matsusaka Shrine instead. ==Shrine buildings and subsidiary shrines== The main hall is built in the Shinmei-zukuri style. Other structures include the worship hall and office. A _reisha_ shrine dedicated to the war dead from the Meiji Restoration through the Greater East Asia War is located on the grounds. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku'', vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1. Kōgakkan University Press, 1990. ==See also== {{commonscat|Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Ohi Shrine: ** {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von based on wikidata|WD=Q17503039}} (Yamazoe-chō, Matsusaka) ** {{ill|Kunitsu Shrine|de|Kunitsu-Schrein|ja|久尓都神社|zh|久尓都神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link [[co:commons:Category:Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha]][[ja:神戸神館神明社]] [[co:commons:Category:Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha]][[ja:神戸神館神明社]] [[co:commons:Category:Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha]][[ja:神戸神館神明社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha]][[ja:神戸神館神明社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Le Temple Shintô Kanbe-kôtachi-shin'mei-sha - Le bâtiment principal "Le honden".jpg|prefecture={{ill|Matsusaka|WD=Q460829|1=Matsusaka}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.553167|N|136.549889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}}}} [[Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Motoise shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} Moto-Ise Shrines are {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. The first one was {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ({{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}}). ==Overview== {{Shinto shrine | name = '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) | image = [[File:Le Temple Shintô Kanbe-kôtachi-shin'mei-sha - Le bâtiment principal "Le honden".jpg|290px]]
Main hall | location = 1791 Shimomura-chō, Matsusaka, Mie Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|11.4|N|136|32|59.6|E|region:JP-24|type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}}
{{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}}
{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}
{{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677|1=Otokazuchi-no-mikoto}} | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (minor) · former village shrine | established = unknown | shrine_architecture = Shinmei-zukuri | annual_festival = March 28 }} '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Matsusaka City, Mie Prefecture, Japan. It is one of the claimants to the Engishiki-listed "Ohi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, and is traditionally identified as a former Ise shrine site (Moto Ise) where Yamatohime-no-mikoto temporarily stayed at Iino-takamimiya while searching for the place to enshrine Amaterasu. Its former shrine rank was that of a village shrine. ==Shrine name== Historically called "Kan-date Shrine", the Shrine Ledger of 1952 (Shōwa 27) notes that it was also called "Ohi Shrine". However, during the Meiji Restoration, the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was identified with Goshirō Hachiman (now {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}}) in the same city. Therefore, the Shrine Inspector designated the formal name as "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha". According to the Seya Rigon (勢陽俚諺), "Kan-date" was sometimes written as "Kōtatsu". ==Deities enshrined== The following four kami are the primary enshrined deities: * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}} (Amaterasu-ōmikami) * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}} (Toyouke-ōmikami) * {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} (Yamatohime-no-mikoto) * {{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677|1=Otokazuchi-no-mikoto}} (Otokazuchi-no-mikoto, ancestor of the Iitaka county head) On November 10, 1906 (Meiji 39), the following three kami were merged into this shrine; their origins are unclear: * {{ill|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|de|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命|zh|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穗耳命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|1=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花開耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} ==History== The area was established as the *kanda* and *kōbe* of Iitaka District, as recorded in Engishiki, with two *chō* of kanda and 36 households of *kanbe*. According to the Kōtaijingū Gishikichō, during the reign of Emperor Suinin, Yamatohime-no-mikoto brought Amaterasu to this region, where the local clan head Otokazuchi-no-mikoto presented kanda and kōbe lands and built the Iino-takamimiya. The Yamato-hime Chronicle similarly notes that in Suinin 22, Yamatohime-no-mikoto worshipped the Great Kami at Iino-takamimiya for four years, receiving these lands from Otokazuchi-no-mikoto (ancestor of the Wani clan). From then on, offerings were sent to Ise Grand Shrine. The 1906 Shrine Ledger states that a shrine hall was constructed at this site to store these offerings; the Kōbe Office was established to administer local affairs, and Shinmei-sha was enshrined, with the old Kōbe villagers repeatedly rebuilding the shrine. According to the 1952 Shrine Ledger, in 1382 (Kōwa 2), Kitabatake Akiyasu donated four *koku* of shrine lands, a grant upheld by the Kii Domain during the Edo period, but abolished after the Meiji Restoration. A pond on the grounds was once harvested for rushes used to make mats, annually dedicated to Ise Grand Shrine. In the early Meiji period, it was renamed "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha" and ranked as a village shrine. ===Identification as an Engishiki shrine=== The earlier ledger records that it was called "Ohi Shrine". The first to identify it as the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was Deguchi Nobumichi’s *Shinmyōchō Kōshō*, citing the "Shimohino Ogawa" river east of the shrine as the "Shimohino Ogawa" in Engishiki. Because "hino" and "hi" are phonetically similar, this was argued as evidence. Conversely, Imamori Kiyonao in the *Ise Shikinaisha Kenroku* argued that "Shimohino Ogawa" refers to the Atago River flowing west of Kaki-hana Village, not east, and that "Ohi" has no relation to Shimohino. He noted that shrine offices for each of the six districts of Ise Province used the name "Kan-date Shrine" to enshrine the spirit of Ise Grand Shrine, and since Otokazuchi-no-mikoto appears among the enshrined kami here, this shrine served as the guardian for Iitaka’s kōbe and should be identified as a Kokutsu Shrine. He accordingly identified the Engishiki Ohi Shrine with the current Matsusaka Shrine instead. ==Shrine buildings and subsidiary shrines== The main hall is built in the Shinmei-zukuri style. Other structures include the worship hall and office. A _reisha_ shrine dedicated to the war dead from the Meiji Restoration through the Greater East Asia War is located on the grounds. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku'', vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1. Kōgakkan University Press, 1990. ==See also== {{commonscat|Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Ohi Shrine: ** {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}} * Claimants to the Engishiki Kunitsu Shrine: ** {{ill|Kunitotomi Shrine|de|Kunitotomi-Schrein|ja|加世智神社|zh|加世智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitotomi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579678|1=Kunitotomi Shrine}} (merged into Kunitsu Shrine in Ōhirao-chō, Matsusaka) * Former Ise shrine sites (Iino-takamimiya): ** {{ill|Kamiyama Shrine|de|Kamiyama-Schrein|ja|神山神社|zh|神山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyama Shrine|1=kouyamajinja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17503039}} (Yamazoe-chō, Matsusaka) ** {{ill|Kunitsu Shrine|de|Kunitsu-Schrein|ja|久尓都神社|zh|久尓都神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579680|1=Kunitsu Shrine}} ** {{ill|Takino Shinmei Shrine|de|Takino-Shinmei-Schrein|ja|水屋神社|zh|水屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takino Shinmei Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579681|1=Takino Shinmei Shrine}} (merged into Mizuya Shrine, Iitaka) ** {{ill|Hanaoka Shrine|de|Hanaoka-Schrein|ja|花岡神社|zh|花岡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanaoka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579682|1=Hanaoka Shrine}} [[ja:神戸神館神明社]] [[de:Kanbe-Kotachi-Shinmei-Schrein]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|神戸神館神明社|version=88389931|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{everybodywiki link|Kanbe Kotachi Shinmeisha}} [[ja:%E7%A5%9E%E6%88%B8%E7%A5%9E%E9%A4%A8%E7%A5%9E%E6%98%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Commons category|Kanbekôtachi-shin'meisha}} {{wikidata link|Q11590193}} [[Category:Buildings in Matsusaka, Mie]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]] [[Category:Everybody linked]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] The first one was {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ({{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}}). ==Overview== {{Shinto shrine | name = '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) | image = [[File:Le Temple Shintô Kanbe-kôtachi-shin'mei-sha - Le bâtiment principal "Le honden".jpg|290px]]
Main hall | location = 1791 Shimomura-chō, Matsusaka, Mie Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|11.4|N|136|32|59.6|E|region:JP-24|type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}}
{{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}}
{{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}
{{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677|1=Otokazuchi-no-mikoto}} | shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (minor) · former village shrine | established = unknown | shrine_architecture = Shinmei-zukuri | annual_festival = March 28 }} '''Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha''' (神戸神館神明社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Matsusaka City, Mie Prefecture, Japan. It is one of the claimants to the Engishiki-listed "Ohi Shrine" in Iitaka District, Ise Province, and is traditionally identified as a former Ise shrine site (Moto Ise) where Yamatohime-no-mikoto temporarily stayed at Iino-takamimiya while searching for the place to enshrine Amaterasu. Its former shrine rank was that of a village shrine. ==Shrine name== Historically called "Kan-date Shrine", the Shrine Ledger of 1952 (Shōwa 27) notes that it was also called "Ohi Shrine". However, during the Meiji Restoration, the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was identified with Goshirō Hachiman (now {{ill|Matsusaka Shrine|de|Schrein von Matsusaka|ja|松阪神社|zh|松阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka Shrine|1=Matsusaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532402}}) in the same city. Therefore, the Shrine Inspector designated the formal name as "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha". According to the Seya Rigon (勢陽俚諺), "Kan-date" was sometimes written as "Kōtatsu". ==Deities enshrined== The following four kami are the primary enshrined deities: * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Inneres Heiligtum)|ja|皇大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Inner Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kōtai Jingū|WD=Q11581011}} (Amaterasu-ōmikami) * {{ill|Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|de|Geist des Ise-Schreins (Äußeres Heiligtum)|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|伊勢神宮外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirit of the Grand Shrine of Ise (Outer Shrine)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toyōke Daijingū|WD=Q11633343}} (Toyouke-ōmikami) * {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} (Yamatohime-no-mikoto) * {{ill|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|de|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja|乙加豆知命|zh|乙加豆知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579677|1=Otokazuchi-no-mikoto}} (Otokazuchi-no-mikoto, ancestor of the Iitaka county head) On November 10, 1906 (Meiji 39), the following three kami were merged into this shrine; their origins are unclear: * {{ill|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|de|Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命|zh|正勝吾勝勝速日天忍穗耳命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsuhikohikoho Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシホミミ|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q462555}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|1=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花開耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} ==History== The area was established as the *kanda* and *kōbe* of Iitaka District, as recorded in Engishiki, with two *chō* of kanda and 36 households of *kanbe*. According to the Kōtaijingū Gishikichō, during the reign of Emperor Suinin, Yamatohime-no-mikoto brought Amaterasu to this region, where the local clan head Otokazuchi-no-mikoto presented kanda and kōbe lands and built the Iino-takamimiya. The Yamato-hime Chronicle similarly notes that in Suinin 22, Yamatohime-no-mikoto worshipped the Great Kami at Iino-takamimiya for four years, receiving these lands from Otokazuchi-no-mikoto (ancestor of the Wani clan). From then on, offerings were sent to Ise Grand Shrine. The 1906 Shrine Ledger states that a shrine hall was constructed at this site to store these offerings; the Kōbe Office was established to administer local affairs, and Shinmei-sha was enshrined, with the old Kōbe villagers repeatedly rebuilding the shrine. According to the 1952 Shrine Ledger, in 1382 (Kōwa 2), Kitabatake Akiyasu donated four *koku* of shrine lands, a grant upheld by the Kii Domain during the Edo period, but abolished after the Meiji Restoration. A pond on the grounds was once harvested for rushes used to make mats, annually dedicated to Ise Grand Shrine. In the early Meiji period, it was renamed "Kambe Kōtachi Shinmei-sha" and ranked as a village shrine. ===Identification as an Engishiki shrine=== The earlier ledger records that it was called "Ohi Shrine". The first to identify it as the Engishiki-listed Ohi Shrine was Deguchi Nobumichi’s *Shinmyōchō Kōshō*, citing the "Shimohino Ogawa" river east of the shrine as the "Shimohino Ogawa" in Engishiki. Because "hino" and "hi" are phonetically similar, this was argued as evidence. Conversely, Imamori Kiyonao in the *Ise Shikinaisha Kenroku* argued that "Shimohino Ogawa" refers to the Atago River flowing west of Kaki-hana Village, not east, and that "Ohi" has no relation to Shimohino. He noted that shrine offices for each of the six districts of Ise Province used the name "Kan-date Shrine" to enshrine the spirit of Ise Grand Shrine, and since Otokazuchi-no-mikoto appears among the enshrined kami here, this shrine served as the guardian for Iitaka’s kōbe and should be identified as a Kokutsu Shrine. He accordingly identified the Engishiki Ohi Shrine with the current Matsusaka Shrine instead. ==Shrine buildings and subsidiary shrines== The main hall is built in the Shinmei-zukuri style. Other structures include the worship hall and office. A _reisha_ shrine dedicated to the war dead from the Meiji Restoration through the Greater East Asia War is located on the grounds. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Shikinaisha Research Association, ed. ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku'', vol. 6: Tōkaidō 1.
